summary_text: **Verdict** Do **not** introduce Hermes-style external memory into FUGUE/Kernel now. Default decision is `NO`, because it creates a second de facto state plane unless you prove strict non-authority behavior in code and operations. **Why this is a hard no (today)** - Authority boundary risk: Kernel already has canonical state (`bootstrap receipt`, `runtime ledger`, `compact artifact`, `runtime health`). External memory will drift into “decision input authority” and create split-brain behavior during recovery and health checks. - Verification burden is disproportionate: you must re-validate phase gates, recovery paths, consensus evidence, and failure handling under stale/partial/offline memory. This is not a feature patch; it is a control-plane contract expansion. - Hidden complexity is operational, not just technical: provenance tracking, TTL/expiry semantics, conflict arbitration, replay safety, and sidecar outage behavior all increase MTTR unless fully engineered. **Decision thresholds to reconsider (all required)** 1. Enforced read-only contract: external memory cannot write or mutate runtime authority fields (`phase`, `owner`, `status`, `next_action`) at code level. 2. Canonical-first conflict rule: on any mismatch, Kernel canonical artifacts always win, with mandatory conflict logging. 3. Provenance+TTL on every retrieval unit: `run_id`, source path, timestamp, expiry; expired items auto-rejected. 4. Measured net gain: at least `30%` reduction in resume-time context payload with no quality regression. 5. Reliability non-regression: no drop in phase-gate pass rate or recovery success across a meaningful run window. 6. Failure isolation proof: sidecar outage must not block `doctor`, `recover-run`, or health classification. If any one threshold fails, keep Hermes out of Kernel.
